transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hey Sam.
You're muted.
Oh, there you are.
Sorry, how are you doing?
Good, how are you doing?
How was your weekend?
Yeah, it was good, thanks.
Yeah, I'm sort of, I was in Stockholm last week, I flew home for the weekend, and now I'm back in Stockholm this week, so.
Oh, wow.
It's been, yeah, pretty, it's been like back and forth, thrashy, but it's all good.
Yeah, it seems like it, for sure.
How are you doing?
Happy Monday.
Thank you, yeah, I'm good.
Yeah, I've just, my wife and I were, were dog-sitting my friend's Bernice mountain dog.
Oh, wow, that's a handful.
Yeah, for like, a week and a half, and, but we already have, we have two dogs and chihuahua mixes, so like, we're just trying to like, hide all of like, the small toys that like, the.
Bernese could swallow.
So yeah, just kind of house-proofing.
So yeah, George, I'm a dog-sitting a Bernese mountain dog in a couple days.
It's big task.
It's big task.
You're a good friend for that.
I think I am, yeah.
Amazing.
How are you both doing, Johanna?
It's good to see you.
Sam, how are you?
Good.
Good to see you, too.
It's the first time I can really join this, so.
Yeah, yeah.
It's good to, yeah.
Yeah, I don't think we've officially met, so it is good to meet you.
I'm a director here at GrowthX.
I'm, yeah, supporting the Lovable account.
So yeah, happy to, happy to meet you.
Cool.
Good to meet you, too.
I'm, I'm running part of product marketing, basically.
Thanks.
Yeah.
Yeah, I mean, a kind of work on our website, on like the content on our website.
I, yeah, you obviously reached out on the, um, uh, on the, like, channel as well, um, about, like, e-works and stuff.
Exactly.
Fantastic.
There's a few places we can go.
Sam, I had some questions that came up from the meeting last week and then over the weekend as well.
So happy to jump into each one of those, which is basically the review process, the organic growth strategy proposal and kind of methodology behind that.
And then what that looks like, like if we actually want to action on it moving forward.
And then the progress on editorial and guides from when we last spoke.
So I'm happy to jump in wherever if there's nothing else on top of mind for you.
Yeah, cool.
And also, like I've started looking through the docs a bit more, a bit more detail.
Like I looked at your Looker dashboard as well, which was helpful.
So thank you for pulling that together.
The impression I get, I'll just start with the Looker dashboard.
The impression I get is that we are like, we're seeing week on week growth from the, like all of the content that we've produced in terms of the amount of traffic.
I think that's generating as well as the like conversions that are coming off the back of that.
We should carry that down to purchases, but like I assume that it's going to align pretty well with purchases.
Yeah, I don't think I had much more on that actually.
Are you expecting like that to continue?
you expecting it to increase?
Are you expecting a slight plateau off at some point?
Yeah, so there's two things, but I think the idea there is the foundation is set and like it's working, right?
It's slowly starting to compound both on the editorial guides and the templates, but at five articles per week, like that progress, it's still going to continue.
But we would rather accelerate that, especially for a brand as big as Lovable.
And when we actually want to start beginning to track real dollar value to the business within a few months, like to really make an impact on your signups.
Um, we...
We...
We...

We want to accelerate, that was the proposal from last week, is like, let's treat this next couple months as a sprint to where we execute against those 400 opportunities that we showed, all low difficulty, low complexity.
Let's remove the review process, right, and so set the foundation, attacking all the opportunities, and then whatever's working will double down, improve with conversion in mind.
But we really wanted to accelerate, because what we've seen with clients, like Augment Code in the past, when we upped it to 15 articles per week, 20 articles per week, it didn't cannibalize.

And, like, it really just showed us what was working and what was resonating, because every piece of content we do still goes through the review filters that we have in place, which are essentially the artifacts that we've made that your team has reviewed and aligned on, right?
And so that was the idea and the goal here, for us to kind of improve the impact we're having, just continue to accelerate.
We're really excited, but that was kind of the thinking behind it.
Yeah.
That makes sense.
And I think in terms of like adding new content to Lovable, that is the clear way to do it.
The only thing that's like holding us back, I guess, is understanding the risk of Google sort of like identifying this as programmatic content or poor content to any extent.
And then we get penalized off the back of that.
And I think we've seen some others in the industry that happened to.
So I guess it would be good to, sorry, there's two things here.
One is like, it's good to, it'd be great to just like get your input on that.
I think you've probably talked about it before.
So apologies, you might be repeating yourself.
The second thing as well here is we're also going to start accelerating work on the like technical side of SEO.
And then, yeah.
And there's also doing the work on like improving the core pages on our site.
starting on the page and then making improvements beyond there.
Because there's just like, so we have so many big pages on our site, which just have the content, actual content on them.
So yeah, that's the other piece.
But yeah, let's maybe just focus on the piece that GrowthX are working on.
And like, put me at peace, make me like, I get your point.
And like, I'm fully on board.
If we, if I had like a guarantee that we would not be penalized by people for this, then I would have, you know, I would have said yes last week.
And we would have said yes last week.
But yeah, that's the thing that's holding me back right now.
Yeah, yeah.
And no, I mean, I totally get where you're coming from.
Like, that's a valid concern.
But yeah, the way that we're thinking about potentially like mitigating some of that risk.
Like, so I guess, yeah, if you, if you kind of like.
Just peruse our content OS and Airtable.
Like you'll see that we have like a few different topic clusters.
And then also in those specific topics, there are also varying content formats that we could take.
And then of course, then another element is also like timing of the actual posts.
like on one hand, it's like, I guess the way I'm thinking about it is that we can kind of have some variety in terms of like, what topics are we talking about?
What format do they have?
When, which days of the week are we posting them on?
Like, obviously like we wouldn't want to post everything.
We wouldn't want to post like five listicles on Monday.
Like that's on the same, on similar topics.
Like that's not what we'd want to do.
We'd want to like alternate in terms of like the, the subject matter.
And so we could have a very clear, essentially like a formula to the formula of like, okay, like this week we're doing a mix of listicles.
How to's comparisons.
How
And it deep dives across multiple topics so that we're still, so that everything we're doing on a weekly basis is still fresh and original and unique.
And then like the following week, we could do like a slightly different mix of topics.
And so, because I think, yeah, with Google, they want to make sure that we're not just, they want to, number one, make sure that everything that we're doing is valuable.
And I think some of that value comes in the variety of content, trying to target different audience, target different keywords.
So that's at least how I'm thinking about it.
But I don't know, George, if you wanted to add anything, anything else to that.
Yeah, no, no, I think that was great.
That's pretty much what I was thinking.
I would say like with clients in the past, that has worked.
It's because we executed against a plan that we kind of thought pretty thoroughly through.
And this is exactly the air table that Megan was speaking of.
Like we already have the ideas of what types of content that we want to create, right?
And just because we're increasing.
That doesn't mean necessarily that it's putting us at risk because it's still going through the same process that we already run, which is not just like one-shotting everything.
Outside looking in, looks like a one-shot, but every one of our – this is just like – I could show you quickly in the workspace here.
But every one of our articles that we generate here is in Lovable's workspace.
Let's go here.
And we have a bunch of different pipelines.
So the way each type of content is built is unique as well within the workflow itself.
And then these are all the steps that actually – that the workflow runs before we even have it looked at by one of our experts.
And then we go through additional post-processing process.
Like the work being done here is really, really extensive.
So I think that is the biggest mitigator of risk in and of itself.
And we can just scale up instead of five per day if we want the first
Two weeks to see, let's go to 10 the first week, 20 the second week, and then get up to that 25 weekly.
We're monitoring daily anyway, all the signals that all these articles are getting.
And so if we see something taking a little too long, we're not getting impressions or clicks, then we can go and vet that immediately and act accordingly.
So it's not like we just want to press play and send five articles per day all at the same time, all the same type of quality.
Like it's targeting different audiences, it's targeting different topic clusters, and we're monitoring it consistently.
And so when we ramped up with, again, augment code in the past, ramping to 15 and 25 was not a problem at all.
We have another client that we're doing 50 per week for, which is a little bit more unique, but we're familiar with ramping up the quality.
How do you know if we're being finalized or not?
It's a good question.
I would monitor to see if the time that it's taking for these articles to get published published is at all being affected, like the average time.
And

So if there's articles that are sitting an abnormally long amount of time without getting impressions or clicks, the first couple of early signals that we look at, I would go in and try to understand why.
And we have a technical team who comes in and checks all the site health daily, weekly, whenever we kind of set check-ins as well.
So we would just monitor that daily pretty much as we're ramping up because it's a critical time.
Yeah, and we can also use, like, between GSC and Semrush, can also track indexing and actual ranking of content.

So, like, if we're seeing maybe that, like, some rankings are dropping or, like, even just the site's overall, like, ranking and authority is dropping or being affected in some way, then that would also sort of be a flag for us that, like, okay, like, maybe we are being penalized in this way.
So we have a lot of different tools to make sure that everything is going according to plan.
And because we're constantly monitoring these data sources, we can easily...
Make adjustments along the way.
And what's like an adjustment?
Is it just slowing down the rate of publishing?
It could be, yeah, it could be slowing down.

It could be taking a closer look at, like, it could be taking a closer look at maybe, like, some of the specific content that maybe fell in SERP and looking at, like, okay, like, if this listicle about, like, AI, like, apps inspiration is dropping, like, maybe doing, like, a content audit to see, like, okay, well, like, is there an issue of that maybe it's too similar to something else that you've already published?
Is Google getting confused by something?
So it would kind of depend on the post itself, but we would be able to sort of drill down into, like, okay, like, what is the post that's being affected?
And then now let's do a content audit to see if there's a clear reason for why this might be happening.
Correct.
And to that point, like, when we...
Our first instinct when we go in and check to see what's wrong is content quality, number one.
Why is this article not performing the way we expected it to?
And so we default to content quality.
And downstream of that, or the inputs for our content quality, is everything we've already reviewed, like the artifacts, all the different audience and personas.

And so that's what makes me feel pretty confident in this process is if we're still providing high-quality, valuable, useful, differentiated content, that's our number one thing that we solve for all the time.
And so removing the review layer doesn't reduce the quality of our workflows to the point where it's not valuable, not differentiated, et cetera, et cetera.
We're just simply increasing the volume here.
And so as long as we make sure the content is quality, it's solving real problems, answering real questions that people are searching for, that to me is why I feel confident in scaling up.
Because I guess if we do hurt, like...
Domain authority and stuff.
It's not necessarily just one article or two that costs that.
It's probably a number over time that causes that.
And it's also not something you revert just by removing one or two.
It's like an accumulative thing.
Right.
No, yeah.
Is that true?
Yeah.
Yeah, you're absolutely right.
Yeah.
Sorry, am I?
Sorry, you have?
Yeah, I mean, I'm just like, it's not that one-to-one.
Like, okay, we saw that it decreased, so it was this article, let's remove it.
I mean, it's a little bit more like a thing that will happen gradually, I would assume.
So that's just something that concerns me in a sense here.
And I wonder also, we do this purely based on SEO, I guess, on search data.
Do we want to take an angle of some sort of, like, use...
Cases that we see that users like try to build and stuff like that, do we want to infuse this with more data or is it, maybe it makes total sense to just purely go from like search data?
No, we're totally on the same, like they're both aligned and we can do both at the same time in a lot of cases.
So the how-to types of content, how to build certain things, obviously some of these are not applicable, right, we're going to prune.
But the how-to build, whether it's websites or actual apps and templates, we can create content to target these similar keywords and so that speaks of the use case and that we can go deeper as well.
But this is more so the initial set of low complexity, low competition, high volume content that we wanted to start with.
And you
You Sam, a dashboard where you saw the performance.
Like, where is that dashboard?
I'm just kind of curious.
Maybe it would be good to share it.
Yeah, can give you the link afterwards.
It's just...
Yeah, sounds good.
Yeah, I can just share my screen real quick if that...
If we have something else to cover, we can start with that.
I can look after.
I think this is important, too.
All right, let me just give...
Okay, can you see my screen?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Okay, cool.
Yeah, so there are a few different tabs here, but so this is, like, non-branded clicks.
But the stuff that is GrowthX specific, so we are looking at guides, URL clicks and impressions, as you can see by a landing page, and kind of go on a week-by-week basis.
And then the same thing applies for templates, similar format.

And then conversion events, which actually is something, Sam, that I did want to check in on with you, just because we were looking at, so this is essentially, these are all the events specific to templates that we've done.
This is overall, you can kind of like, whoops, kind of look at it by like date range, if you just want to look at like November, sometimes looking can be a little slow to load.
But so this is like total event count, and then purchases are, it's a little lower, but then you have key events.
But I think, yeah, I was still actually looking for some clarity on what is included in key events.
I know that I think some of that is, some of that are like the purchases and first visits, but I was just curious, like, what are those other key events?
But also, like, what you all feel like is the most important metric to track?
Like, I would imagine purchases is definitely up there, but just curious.
There are like other ones that we should try and pull out to track.
Yeah, so I think the key events includes sign-ups, which is, and I think it potentially includes certain page views as well.
Purchases are really like the key thing, right?
And that's what we need to pull.
And it's quite interesting, actually, because you can see that over the last week, purchases have actually started to pick up.
I know it's only a small number, but if you set up to like the last 30 days or whatever, you can start to see their purchases are starting to pick up.
that, which is great, which is great to see.
Obviously, we need that to continue growing some way.
Yeah, it's good.
Yeah, sure.
Yeah, and yeah, what we've also, yeah, yeah, because I think when we first looked that it was looking like guides were kind of.
Driving the most in terms of purchases, but it looks like now templates are also doing that as well.
So yeah, it's about, they're about like, even in terms of performance.
Which, yeah, which makes sense.

And like, I love your idea, Joanna, there on like, use case specific content, because the templates, that's what they're solving for, is like, you're giving someone a starting point to actually go in and build.
You're giving them a place to land that teaches them how to build and who this is for.
We're going to continue to optimize the templates with Sebastian and the team to make them more user-friendly, and then be featured on the main site as a starting point.
But then creating also just like full-blown editorial content, breaking down that entire process could be helpful as well.
And so we're super open to it.
I think just like the main thing here is we set the foundation for our workflows and the review process to streamline all that.
And now we get like really high quality content out with not too much lift from our team.
still have our experts come in and look at every piece.

But now is the time, like if I asked you a month ago to ramp up, I would not have been confident that we could do it sustainably because there's so much dev time that went into actually building out all these workflows.
But this is just now another piece of like, now that we're seeing the numbers increase, if we just double down, that's what makes us so excited about the compounding effect here.

Yeah, I think, and the, just the, the biggest argument for me internally on this as well, like in my own mind too, is that actually only 2% of the SEO acquisition that we're achieving is non-brand right now.
So like, you're not really actually going to break much if you do break something.
Okay, I, I definitely feel better, uh, with like a slower ramp, um, just see if this continues to snowball, um, for your, for your awareness, like, so we're, we're just,
This week, we're going start working with an SEO contractor who is going to effectively project manage all of the technical work that we need to improve on the site.
Nice.
And we're hopefully getting some web dev time to help with that.
Although that's not confirmed, but I just chatted to Cece.
But she's pushing for it.
So I'm going to chat with them as well.
So I just want to get another opinion on it.
But then I'll come back to you.
Yeah, totally.
And let us know, however, if there's anywhere that we can help there as well.
For one of our other clients, Brex, we're going and recommending an end-to-end rebuild or refresh of their sanity and how they think they're publishing and how they actually interact with their site.
And so we have dev resources as well that we're happy to take a look at or just like second opinion.
You know, it would make you feel better looking at what the other contractor.
Yeah, I guess.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, cool.
We can, what are you now?
Cool.
Okay, and then just so we have a few more minutes here.
So I just want to wrap up two more things.
One, I just want to make sure we're not being blocked after the end of this week because of the holidays.
All of us are pretty much on a quiet week starting, let's see, next Monday, right, Megan?
Yeah, so it's basically, we won't be publishing the week of December 29th is basically what the quiet week means.
But obviously, if things come up, like, we'll still be available.
But we're just kind of trying to give some of our team a little bit of a break for the holidays.
Okay, cool.
And so there we'll probably go async on the meeting as well.
I just wanted to let you all know ahead of that.
I'm flying again this Friday.
We have our time, so maybe we can coordinate a time on Thursday.
I'm free, like, this sort of time on Thursday as well, if that works for you guys.
Yeah, I mean, yeah, that works for me, but, yeah.
We can feel, if that works for you both, then, I tell you what, let's chat async, so, you know, think it's your, your time aligned as well.
Cool.
But, yeah, it's Friday, I'm going be flying again.
Yeah.
No problem.
Busy man.
And then, the only other thing was the review process, Sam.
I know you mentioned reviewing, like, 10,000 words is not realistic.
Completely agree.
And what we did, and we're happy to redo any of this, or help make it as easy as possible for you as well.
The only reason we got to this point where we've been comfortable just, like, sending these are the five things we're executing on.

And going ahead and publishing is because we already had Rachel and Fern and the team review every artifact that we created, which are the steps in our workflow, and also the sample output, which was a calibration piece where we got style and tone.
Drilled in, we updated with all their feedback and information, and then the first iterations of like 15 of our first articles, they were pretty heavy with feedback back and forth.
And so it was only through that process that we got to a point where now we were pretty autonomous in publishing because we built up the trust with them.
And so I totally understand it's like jumping in new, so I'm happy to run you through anything that we've done before to like understand how the process is running.
And then would love, obviously, like it's your baby now, so like you'd love your feedback and input, and we'd be happy to implement that into our process too.
Okay, I'm definitely not the person to be feeding back on like tone and...
No, that's cool, yeah, yeah.
Johanna, I think you'll be great to get your like eyes on this at some point.
And then I think that CC was pushing that we get like some sort of like tone of voice documentation together as well in general.
So maybe like we can review it once we have, let's just like go ahead with the ones for the next couple of weeks.
And then once we have that tone of voice documentation, I think, you know, it'll be good for you to, you'll probably be working closely with that tone of voice documentation.
And then we can have like a review and refresh of the, of the tone work that you've put in to the QueryTex content.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I mean, you guys already have a tone of voice document.
I know.
But we don't have one internally.
So that's me.
We can share this with you.
basically like distilled down from our conversations, right?
Like what are Lovable's branding guidelines?
How do we want to sound?
What does Fern sound like?
What does CC think about this?
And we turned it into like this living document that we feed in to our workflows.
And so it's examples of good and bad, what.
To say what not, to say how to mention Lovable, grounded non-salesy way, when talking about AI in general, I'd actually share content.
I have access to this, actually, or I think I found it, at least.
Yeah, but we are crafting something internally as well.
So, cool.
Amazing.
Yep, it's all on Notion as well in our workspace, so feel free to use it and take inspiration or however it's helpful.
Cool.
Amazing.
So, in terms of next steps, then, how does it sound if I just synthesize the, like, the growth strategy proposal, right?
I think it's in, like, two different places right now where the really fast, accelerated version, which, to me, feels more project-based.
It's basically, like, 380 assignments that we think are really, really good to start on.
And then the regular increase, like, 3X increase in editorial would kick in thereafter.
And so I can make that pretty clear and concise.
Is there anything that would be helpful for you in the meantime, Sam, that while I'm prepping that?
I don't think so, but just so I understand this, if you're not publishing the week after next, then would we actually delay any acceleration until after that?

Let me confirm, because we love working with you guys, and so I think because we already have a review in such a good spot, I just want to create a backlog of content this week and next week, so we actually don't have to hold off on publishing.
But let me confirm with the team, but ideally, I wanted to keep moving fast here, and so...
Well, we've signed off on the next 20 articles, I think hopefully...
Correct.
Well, if we did that over the next, after this week, I don't know, maybe we need to sign off on some more, but just have a chat internally and let us know what's best.
Definitely.
Fantastic.
This was helpful.
We'll let you know on those three things as well.
Love it.
All right, yeah, hopefully we'll to this quite quickly.
Thanks, folks.
Yeah, excited to talk soon.
Thank you.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[To discuss accelerating content production and address Lovable's concerns about quality and risk.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=124.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**GrowthX proposed a content sprint**—ramping from 5 to 25 articles/week to attack \~400 low-difficulty opportunities and accelerate growth.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=239.0)
  - [**Lovable's primary concern is Google penalizing the site** for low-quality or programmatic content, which could harm domain authority.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=309.0)
  - [**GrowthX's mitigation plan** includes content variety (topics, formats), a robust production workflow, and daily monitoring of GSC/Semrush for performance signals.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=412.0)
  - [**Decision deferred:** Sam will consult a new SEO contractor for a second opinion before committing to the acceleration plan.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1244.0)

## Topics

### Content Performance & Acceleration Proposal

  - [**Current Performance:** The Looker dashboard shows week-on-week growth in traffic and conversions (sign-ups, purchases) from the current 5 articles/week.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=168.0)
  - [**Acceleration Rationale:** GrowthX proposed a content sprint to accelerate growth and drive more significant business impact (e.g., sign-ups, purchases).](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=204.0)
      - [**Goal:** Attack \~400 low-difficulty, low-complexity opportunities identified in Airtable.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=239.0)
      - [**Method:** Increase production to 25 articles/week, removing the manual review process now that workflows are calibrated.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=239.0)
      - [**Precedent:** Past clients (e.g., Augment Code) saw no cannibalization when scaling to 15–20 articles/week.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=266.0)

### Lovable's Risk Concerns & Mitigation

  - [**Primary Concern:** Google penalizing the site for low-quality or programmatic content, which could harm domain authority.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=309.0)
  - [**Mitigation Strategy:**](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=412.0)
      - [**Content Variety:** Mix topics, formats (listicles, how-to's, comparisons), and publication timing to avoid patterns Google might flag.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=442.0)
      - [**Robust Workflow:** The production process is extensive, including expert review and post-processing, ensuring quality is maintained at scale.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=550.0)
      - [**Daily Monitoring:** Track GSC/Semrush for early penalty signals (e.g., slow indexing, ranking drops) to enable rapid adjustments.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=682.0)
  - [**Adjustments for Poor Performance:**](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=723.0)
      - [Slowing the publication rate.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=723.0)
      - [Conducting a content audit on underperforming articles to diagnose issues (e.g., keyword cannibalization).](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=723.0)

### Review Process & Tone of Voice

  - [**Current Process:** GrowthX publishes autonomously, a trust built through heavy feedback on initial artifacts and articles.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1434.0)
  - [**New Review:** Sam agreed the current volume (10k words) is unrealistic for manual review.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1417.0)
      - [Johanna will take over tone/style feedback.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1488.0)
      - [This will align with Lovable's new internal tone of voice documentation.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1493.0)
  - [**GrowthX's Tone Doc:** GrowthX's existing Notion doc, created from past feedback, is available for reference.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1538.0)

### Holiday Schedule

  - [**Publishing Pause:** No new content will be published the week of Dec 29.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1331.0)
  - [**Team Availability:** GrowthX team will be available async.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1331.0)
  - [**Content Backlog:** GrowthX will create a backlog of approved articles this week and next to maintain publishing momentum.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1622.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**George:** Synthesize the growth strategy proposal into a concise document.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1589.0)
  - [**Sam:** Consult the new SEO contractor for a second opinion on the acceleration plan's risks.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1244.0)
  - [**Sam:** Share the Looker dashboard link with Johanna.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=960.0)
  - [**GrowthX:** Confirm if the content backlog will allow for continuous publishing through the holiday week.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1622.0)
  - [**All:** Schedule the next sync for Thursday or coordinate async.](https://fathom.video/share/4Ji9VmwJuUzd6ry4WQ_vmzrqMyXPV6dh?tab=summary&timestamp=1375.0)

action_items.description: Send Looker dashboard link to Johanna
Consult SEO contractor re: ramp-up risk; report back to George & Megan
Schedule Thu sync w/ Sam & George (async)
Synthesize growth strategy proposal; send to Sam & Johanna
Confirm w/ team quiet-week publishing plan; update Sam & Johanna
